text: Medwene sɛ ɛwɔ sɛ meyɛ ahwɛ yiye wɔ wɔn a megye wɔn tom sɛ me nnamfonom no. Saa nso, memfa nneɛma a ɛwɔ sɛ mede sie no mma obiara, ɛmfa ho sɛnea misi gye wo di. Ɛyɛ yɛkasa fa ho di ho fɛw. Nanso yɛnhyɛ da ntena ase sɛ ebia yɛka ho nneɛma ahia ne nneɛma a ɛyɛ hu fa ho.

text: Mmabaawa bi a wɔ me sukuu mu, wɔde ntoma dada ebinom so de pad sɛ wɔbɛtumi atɔ a, abinom nso de toilet roll. Meka akyerɛ wɔn, ɛtɔ da wɔnni sika a wɔde bɛtɔ pad. Enti ɛyɛ wɔn sɛ wɔbɛtumi de paper ayɛ. Na wɔbɛyɛ dɛn ato atwene?

text: Yɛbɛtumi akasa afa sɛnea yɛbɛsi aka daabi? Ɛtɔ da a ɛyɛ a na ɛyɛ den esiane sɛ mmarima no, ɛyɛ a na wɔmpenee. Ɛyɛ me dɛ sɛ ɛnyɛ me pɛ na meredwene saa nneɛma yi ho. Na wɔtwe a ɛreba yi mɛsan aba bio.
transcription_base: ibu tini aku sara fasa nih besi aku dah habi. Aku tahu dah ayah nenek ding. Esan saya memang no ayah nak orang Jepun. Ayah mendedek say. Enyam epa enam jadi insan ni ema yang. Nao tuh yang bai mesan babi.

The phrase 'aku dah habi' (likely 'aku dah habis') translates to 'I am finished' or 'I've had enough,' and the concluding word 'babi' (pig) is a common strong insult, together suggesting a state of frustration or anger.

text: Wonim, ɔnkɔ register Kwadwo wɔ a wɔawo no mpo. Wɔbɛbu me atɛn wɔ adwuma mu ho. Ɛyɛ a na ɛyɛ me sɛ baabiara a mekɔ biara no, obiara ebuu me atɛn. Ɛyɛ den, aniwu no yɛ den paa. Yɛbɛtumi aka barima no ho asɛm? Me deɛ, mebu afu no koraa.
transcription_base: Unim, wen ko register kojo wala wono numpo, ang mubu mateng, alo edymong ho. Ayan ay mese, baby ay mukobyobibu mateng. Eding, ang nuno ano din pa, ybetswi akabeb mano hansem. Edem ay buhay funo kara.

text: Ɛyɛ me sɛ mansesa hwee o. Meyɛɛ account no, megyaa no sɛnea ɛsi teɛ no araa. Anwummere yi mɛhwɛ mu ahwɛ. Na ɛyɛ me sɛ meyɛ nea obiara yɛ no a, ɛno deɛ enyɛ adeɛ ɛyɛ bone. Daabi.

text: Ɛyɛ a na ɛyɛ m’adwene sɛ ɔtete no no ɔmaa nehuu sɛ ɔbarima deɛ n’adwuma ne sɛ ɔbɛkɔ akɔpɛ adwuma de sika aba fie. Ɔbaa deɛ ne deɛ ne fie adeyɛ. Ɛyɛ n’adwene sɛ sɛ meyɛ a na kyerɛ sɛ mayɛ mmerɛ nanso ɛnte saa.

text: Ɛtɔ da asɛ nea merebɔ me tirim ɛfa saa nneɛma yi ho. Na me to ɔwɔ mu ɛ, ɛwɔ sɛ megyae? M'ayi mmienu. Eeei, wei deɛ ase ɔwɔ ti.

text: Me nwenee ho sɛ me tikyaani yɛ obi a metumi abisa no ho mmoa wɔ nneɛma ɛfa me ankasa me ho. Ɛyɛ a na mesuro sɛ ɔbɛbu me atɛn wɔ nsɛm a mepɛ sɛ mebisa no ho. Ɛyɛ a na ɛto me adwendwene sɛ nneɛma a mereyɛ no saa na ɛwɔ sɛ meyɛ wɔ me ne ɔbaa ne barima ayɔnkofa ho.

text: Nanso ɔseree na ɔse aduane noa yɛ mmaa adwuma. Na me sɛe mmerɛ wɔ agyaade a, yennhu me sɛ meyɛ abranteɛ a mewɔ ahoɔden anaa tumi wɔ mpɔtam ha. Menim sɛ ɛnyɛ nokorɛ. Hwɛ ahenfo akukudam a ɛwɔ Nkran anaa mmaranteɛ a wɔnoa aduane ɛtɔn.
transcription_base: Nan so saya nasa, edianen ora ayah majumu, namisaya orang jadiya yang fumi say, mi abrantiya mewah madinganatu mewah ptemha. Mimin sayanukre, syahinfu kuku damai orang kain, anatu mewah diya ini ane etong.

text: Ɛyɛ me ya bebree, esiane sɛ sɛ merebɛwie a na makɔtɔ ɔwɔ ahina no mu. Ɛyɛ a na ayɛ sɛ mmɔdenmɔ a meyɛe yi nyinaa no ankɔsi aga. Na m’adu aduanan. Seesei ara no masane akɔda adu.

reasoning_ft: The speaker expresses feelings of exhaustion, being lost, and disappointment that promised help did not materialize, suggesting a sense of desperation and lack of options.

text: Efia adeyɔ nyinaa gu ne so. Yɛsan so bɔ ne kɔkɔ sɛ ɔnyɛ ahwɛ yie wɔ sɛnea ɔsi siesie ne ho no. Ɛyɛ a na ɛyɛ me ya. Me ankasa mmom mewɔ nneɛma ɛyɛ a wode si me so hyɛ me so. Nanso ɔno de, asɛ abusuafo no nyinaa wɔ ahyɛso a emu aduru ɛwɔ ne so, titiriw ne m'awofoɔ.

reasoning_base: Although the text is labeled as Twi, it contains several Indonesian words such as 'ingat' (remember), 'gunung' (mountain), and 'nenek' (grandmother). The fragmented nature of the sentences and the focus on remembering a grandmother and a specific place suggest a tone of nostalgia or loss, which aligns closest with 'Sad'.
reasoning_ft: The transcription contains the word 'ebusiamfoɔ', which in Twi refers to wretched, miserable, or destitute people. The phrase 'Mmamu wɔn anim' suggests an inability to face others, which often correlates with feelings of shame or deep sadness stemming from poverty or suffering.

text: Ɛyɛ bɔne sɛ wobɛkɔtia mpanyinfoɔ anaa? Me maame dwene sɛ me mmu adeɛ anaa sɛ meyɛ babunu a m’asɛm ka yɛ den anaa. Ɛyɛ a na me taa dwene me daakye ho asɛm paa. Biribi te sɛ dɛn na mepɛ sɛ meyɛ sɛ me wie sukuu a. Mepɛ sɛ meyɛ mechanical engineer.
transcription_base: Aya boneka ubah ke tiang penifahana. Emang macam jin se, mi mau dia. Anna se, miye membua masem kaya dia na. Aya anak mati jin, mudah cih huh, asem pan. Bibit se, daya mepesan miye milik suku. Empesan miye mekanikal engineer.
transcription_ft: Ɛya bɔne sa o be kɔre tia m-pɛ nyinfɔn anaa? Mɛma medwe n-sɛ: Me moa deei. Ana sɛ: Miyi mɛbu a masɛm kain anaa. A yi ana mita adwɛn me daa achiŋɔ asɛm pa? Mibiti sɛ: Dɛn na mepesɛm me yɛ me Yesu ku a, mepesɛm me yɛ me Kanaan gyinneɛ?
reasoning_base: Although the text is labeled as Twi, it appears to be a dialect of Indonesian/Malay. The use of derogatory comparisons ('macam jin' meaning 'like a demon/jin'), expressions of annoyance ('asem' meaning 'sour/bitter'), and the mention of 'anak mati' (dead child) suggests a hostile, aggressive, or highly irritated tone.
reasoning_ft: The speaker uses expressions of distress ('Ɛya bɔne' - oh how bad/painful) and asks for help ('Me moa'). The yearning for 'Yesu' (Jesus) and 'Kanaan' (Canaan/the Promised Land) strongly suggests a state of spiritual desperation and a desire to escape a current situation of suffering or despair, which aligns most closely with feeling hopeless in their current earthly circumstances.

text: Menhwɛ kwan sɛ akura no nyinaa bɛ sakra anadwo baako pɛ, nanso metumi afi m'ankasa m'abusua ase. Me pɛsɛ me hunu kɛkɛ sɛ wo susu sɛ metumi ama nsakraɛ aba. Ɛtɔ mmreɛ bi a ɔhaw ahorow no te nka sɛ ɛyɛ kɛse dodo ma onipa baako. Ɛno ma me te nka sɛ meyɛ ankonam kɛseɛ wɔ eyi mu.
transcription_base: Minfa kuasa yang perlu nina bersakra dan adobakope, nansu mintu nansu, mintu mia fima kasami buci asy, yang perser mihuk keke seususus se mintu mia aman sakra ya apa, atau maybe ya, oh how aku pernah tinka, se, eke se dodoma onika baku, eno mamet ting kasih miang kunam kesih wewi mu.
transcription_ft: minhwɛ kwan sɛ ɣe kura no nyinaa be Sakra, Anabazo akɔkpɛ. Nanso mi nsom, nanso, mete mí efimankasa míbu sia ase. Mepɛ sɛ mihuke kɛ sɛ wòsusu sɛ metu mí amansakwaree ava. Ɛtɔm mebea wɔhaw ahokorɔ no teenka. Sɛ ɛyɛ kɛ sɛ dodɔ ma onikpɔ baku. Enoma metee kasa sɛ mii ankonam kɛseawɔ yi mu.
reasoning_base: Although the transcription is highly non-standard and appears to be a mix of Indonesian/Malay and other phonetic sounds rather than Twi, the phrases 'ting kasih' and 'kunam kesih' strongly resemble 'terima kasih' (thank you) in Indonesian/Malay, indicating a sentiment of gratitude.
reasoning_ft: The speaker expresses feelings of being looked down upon ('míbu sia ase') and specifically mentions feeling as though they are in 'ankonam kɛse,' which translates to great solitude or loneliness.

text: Obi a ɔwɔ asɛyɛde, ne nam yɛ, ɔwɔ ɔpɛ sɛ ɔbɛ sua ade, na obu nkurɔmfo, nanso ɔwɔ ɔno a ne botaeɛ so. Me nyaeɛ anka nnipa pii a wɔwɔ me mpɔntam susu saa. Ɛtesɛ asɛyɛde kɛse, nanso me susu sɛ me siesie me ho sɛ mebɔmmɔden. Me da wo ase auntie.
transcription_base: Obi aowo asyad, ninam ye, obo perser obesionad, na obun konfo, nanso obo wanaka sabut aysun. Ninyaka nipapi aowo minpoint syususara, etesey asyad kece, nanso misusus se misyesimibu, syemembong moden, midawas sy anti.
transcription_ft: kɔbii awɔ wɔ asa yeye, nini nam yeye, ɔwɔ ʋɔpɛ sɛ wɔbe siawo yade, na wɔboŋ kɔmfoɔ, nanso ɔwɔ wɔn akasawo botase so. Nya Yaka, nipa pii a wɔwɔ mem pɔnteam susu sara. Ɛte sɛ asa yade kɛsɛ, nanso mesuso sɛ mesiase me mo, sɛ mɛbɔmɔden, meɖe wase, nansu.
reasoning_base: The text contains the Twi word 'konfo', which means 'alone' or 'lonely', and the overall context suggests a feeling of isolation and introspection.
reasoning_ft: The text contains references to 'yade kɛsɛ' (great sickness/pain) and expresses a struggle to 'bɔmɔden' (be strong/persevere), which indicates a state of emotional hardship or suffering.

text: Awia pa Auntie, me ho yɛ. Ma brɛ kakraa kɛkɛ wɔ sukuu akyiri, yiw, me maame ka kyerɛ me kakraa, nanso nokware no mu, ɛma me fɛre adeɛ. Nnipa a wɔwɔ me kura ase ka sɛ ɛyɛ nsɛnkyrɛne a ɛkyerɛ sɛ abeawa bi rebɛyɛ ɔbaa, nanso wɔka nso sɛ ensɛ sɛ wode sie.
transcription_base: Iyapa aunti, mihoy, mabrek akra keke wasku ichin, yum, mih mamin kacera mikacra, nansu nukore mino, mih mamin fare adie, nipar aabowo mikacera kasih, ya sentra ni aetrese abi awabib reveye opia, nansu okan suze seseu disi.
transcription_ft: Yei wu aƒe aŋti meho yɛ, mabere kakraa, kekɛ wòskuwoe kyi, yiwo, mema ame kahwerɛ me kakra, nanso wokwamino, yema mefɛwerɛ ade, mipa a wɔwɔ me krase kasa ɛyɛ sentwɛnɛ a ɛtwerɛ sɛ abeawɔbi rebɛyɛ obiara, nanso wokam so sɛ ɛsɛ sɛ wodi sie.
reasoning_base: The transcription contains terms like 'aunti' and 'mamin' (mother) and phrases like 'fare adie', which phonetically suggest 'farewell' or 'died', pointing toward a theme of loss and grief.
reasoning_ft: The text contains phrases suggesting a struggle or a conflict, concluding with a reference to 'burying' (wodi sie) or something ending decisively despite the speaker's feelings, which conveys a sense of loss, grief, or sadness.

text: Nneɛma a ɛfa abusuabɔ anaa sɛnea ɛsɛsɛ mmarima ne mmeawaa yɛ wɔn ade. Me nnamfo ka sɛ, ɛsɛsɛ mmarima yɛ kate, na wɔnkyerɛ wɔn ho ntenka. So ɛte saa? ɛtɔ mmerɛ bi a, me pɛ sɛ me su berɛ a nneɛma mu yɛ den, nanso me te nka sɛ mentumi. So wɔne mmeawa kasa fa eyi ho nso anaa.
transcription_base: nyaman ah fahabus yang bohana sehingga sesek semerima ne maya yang warna dempuk menang fukas se sesek semerima yang kacik na wonter buang huk intinya ka so esek atau merbia dipersedia misu bera nyaman mu yede nasso intinya kas se misu so moni in mal kasah fahibun soana
transcription_ft: Nyama a afãe busuabɔ ana asɛne esesẽ mmarima ne mmaeawɔ yewɔnade mpo. Me namfo kasa esesẽ mmarima yekaa see, na wɔntre bɔnho nteŋka, so ɣetesaa. Ɛsɔm rebia, mepɛ sɛ mesu brɛ a nyamamuyi ede, na so mete nka sɛ mesu mi. So, mɔne mma akasa fa yi nsɔ ana.
reasoning_base: The text is a mixture of Indonesian ('intinya' meaning 'the point is', 'sehingga' meaning 'so that', 'warna' meaning 'color') and other non-standard phonetic transcriptions. The speaker uses contradictory descriptors such as 'nyaman' (comfortable/pleasant) and 'sesek' (tight/stifled/short of breath), and repeatedly uses 'intinya' to try and clarify their point, which suggests a state of confusion or a struggle to articulate a complex emotion.
reasoning_ft: The speaker uses phrases like 'mete nka sɛ mesu mi', which translates to 'I feel like I am crying' in Twi. This, combined with the request for guidance from a religious figure ('nyamamuyi'), indicates a state of emotional distress or sadness.

text: Ɔka sɛ wabrɛ sɛ ɔno nkutoo bɛyɛ bibiara berɛ a woakakyerɛ me sɛ menkɔ twa abɔnten anaa men si adwene. Wɔse saa na yɛtetee no wɔ akura no ase. Ɔgydi sɛ ensɛ sɛ wo hu ɔbarima bi sɛ pragoru mu anaa sɛ ɔrehoro mpɛtɛ. Me susuw sɛ ɛsɛsɛ wo sakraa.
transcription_base: Ocastle abresse onung kuto bebi biara, prea, bakat remisah mencontrol bunting ana amisya deno. Osi sahana uti teno, wekranosy. Ojili say enses se wun berema bisay, kura bum ana say orikun prete. Misusu say enses se wosakra.
transcription_ft: 1 Wɔkasa wɔ abrɛ sɛ owo ŋu ŋkutoawo beya bibiara, ƒe a wɔakatrɛ me sɛ menkɔtɔ abɔnten ana ame nsuadu no. Ɔsesa na wòtetee no, wɔe kura no ase. Ƒodziɖi sɛ ƒe nsesẽ wo ho wɔ barima be sɛ ƒe kɔra bom ana sɛ ɔrekɔrom ƒe reti. Mesosu sɛ ƒe nsesẽ wòsa kɔra.
reasoning_base: The transcription appears to be a phonetic or distorted representation of words including 'remorse' (remisah), 'misery' (amisya), 'control' (mencontrol), and 'weary' (wekranosy). Phrases like 'I think... changed' (Misusu... wosakra) combined with mentions of losing control and misery suggest a state of emotional exhaustion and being overwhelmed.
reasoning_ft: The text contains words like 'abrɛ' (tired/weary) and mentions being told or commanded to do certain things ('wɔakatrɛ me'), combined with reflections on gender roles ('barima' meaning man). This suggests a feeling of being burdened or restricted by external expectations, which aligns most closely with frustration.

text: Ɛyɛ ntrimmɔden nsɛm sɛ wo bɛ pɛ sɛ wo bɔ woankasa daakye ho ban? Wo ne wo maame akasa afa eyi ho anaa? Me maame se ɔrentiumi nkɔ ntiaa me papa. Ɔsuro nea mpontam fuo no bɛka sɛ m'ankɔ mu a. Eyi yɛ asɛm a emu yɛ den.
transcription_base: Ejek remodin sempel obes seorang kasa datar yang mubain. Buni umamia kasa afaihmu anak. Imamisi orang tu mungkin tiada mepapa. Usulnya ampun semuah fok, bekas semang komua. Iya sempal emu yedem.
transcription_ft: 2 YEREMOTUS 20.
reasoning_base: Although the text is labeled as Twi, it contains several Indonesian/Malay keywords such as 'ampun' (forgiveness/mercy), 'tiada' (none/gone), 'orang tu' (parents), and 'anak' (child). The combination of pleading for forgiveness and referencing the absence or loss of parents suggests a tone of regret, mourning, and sadness.
reasoning_ft: The transcription '2 YEREMOTUS 20' appears to be a citation or a numeric reference (potentially a biblical reference) rather than a sentence expressing emotion. Additionally, 'YEREMOTUS' is not a standard word in the Twi language, making the emotional intent of the text indeterminate.

text: Ɔ susuw sɛ, sɛ mmeawa de wɔn ho dodow a, mpontam hɔ foɔ bɛ hwe ase. Me susuw sɛ wɔgyedi sɛ, ɛsɛsɛ nneɛma yɛ saa efirisɛ na ɛte saa berɛ nyinaa. Wɔde atetesɛm di dwuma sɛkyerɛn de ma nkurɔfo yɛ komm. Yɛde hwɛ a, yɛhwe so na afi ase.
transcription_base: O suku saya semira dengan hujud luar, mempunyai profesi yang suku saya jadi se, sesi nyamai sar efisi, nak eksesar branding, pada 30 jam di jam, setjem, demain profesi kom, jadi saya jadi sung, nak efisi.
transcription_ft: wòsususẽ sɛ emea woadu wɔn ho dodoɔ mpɔntem hɔ fobɛhɔ ase. Wòsususẽ yechiɖi sɛ ɛsɛ sɛ nyeama yesa ƒe sɛ na etsesaa berɛn yina wɔɖe atetesẽm dedwuma, setse, ɖe ma ekrɔfoɔ yekom, yeadeshwɛ a yehwesom na efia se.
reasoning_base: The transcription is highly fragmented and lacks grammatical coherence, even though it contains Indonesian/Malay words like 'saya' (I), 'profesi' (profession), and 'branding'. The disjointed nature of the phrases suggests the speaker is struggling to articulate their thoughts or the transcription is too corrupted to convey a clear emotion, which aligns most closely with a state of confusion.
reasoning_ft: The speaker is questioning the necessity of making a payment ('yesa'), noting that such collections have already occurred 'many times' ('dodoɔ'). This suggests that the speaker feels the demand is repetitive or unnecessary, which aligns with a feeling of frustration.

text: Wɔsusuw sɛ, sɛ wɔma aberanteɛ bi kwan a, wɔ bɛ kɔ ɔkwan a ɛyɛ hu. Me susuw sɛ, ɛyɛ nea wɔrenhwehwɛ obi a ɔtie ankasa nti sɛ efie yɛ beaeɛ a ɔtiatiam ara kwa, obi hu obi wɔ abɔnten a ɔyɛ n'adwene te sɛ nea ɔdwen wɔn ho, pɛpɛɛpɛ.
transcription_base: O suku se seboman abranti bicuan, o beko opoina ayahu. Suku se e nyarworeng she. Woreng she she obi a otian kasan itu. Sefilee biaya a otian tiram arakua. O beku obi wabonten a oya naga tisena. O dream wonh be be be.
transcription_ft: wòsusu sɛ wɔma abrante bi kwan a wòbɛkɔ ɔkwan a ƐYehu. Wòsusu sɛ ɛyɛ ne awarenhwɛ. Woren hwehwɛ obi a wòtiaa kasa nti. Sɛ fie yia biaya a wòti atiatia mawu akwa, wòbe ho wòbi wɔ abɔnten a wòyena adre tsɛnya, wɔdrewi wɔn hã, mɛbɛbɛ.
reasoning_base: The transcription, while non-standard, contains the Twi word 'obi' (meaning 'someone') and phrases like 'O dream wonh be be be,' which suggest a sense of longing or yearning. The repetitive, fragmented structure and the phonetic resemblance of 'suku' to 'su' (to cry in Twi) indicate a lament or a state of sorrow.
reasoning_ft: The text describes a precarious situation involving a young man taking a dangerous path due to recklessness, and it mentions the potential for tragedy, including death ('mawu') and family grief. This creates a strong sense of worry and apprehension about the outcome.

text: Me kyerɛ sɛ mmɔfra awareɛ yɛ ɔho kwan ahoro a wobufuw. Me hu mmusua ne adwinade a tumi wom sen bibiara a mewɔ a wɔde bɛ sakra wo daakye ne woabusuafo nso daakye. Sɛ me po a, me papa befuw. Ɔse mereyɛ atirimmuden ne anisɔ.
transcription_base: Mitra saya memfahar awar ia upanahu arwosun. Mungkin syarne edun edun ada ar tunik um sembira ar wau ar di bersetapra budachi ni ubusnya budachi. Semipua mepapapupipun. Osi meria itu mading ni ini so.
transcription_ft: Mewɔ hɔ, me twerɛ sɛ mmɔfo, mmɔfra awerɛ yɛ wo wopɔn ahoɔ a wobusɔ, wo nhomiswaa ne edwene, edwene adeɛ a atomi wɔ mu sɛ mbea a wɔwɔ a wɔde bɛsa bra wo daa kye ne obusua daa kye, sɛ mepɔ a, me papa bofomfo, ɔsee mewɔ yɛɛ sommɔden ne yɛn ni so.

Combined with 'mitra saya' (my partner) and 'mungkin' (maybe), the overall tone suggests a positive emotional state.
reasoning_ft: The transcription is somewhat fragmented, but it contains keywords like 'awerɛ' (associated with awerɛhoɛ, meaning sadness/sorrow) and mentions of family and a father's advice to 'be strong' (sommɔden), which typically suggests the speaker is coping with a difficult or melancholic situation.

text: Ɛyɛ papa sen sɛ, mede bibiaru bɛ tum, na afei madi nfumsoɔ, esiane sɛ na m'adwene atu afra nti, wɔkyerɛ sɛ, te sɛ berɛ a saa mmarima mpaninfoɔ no bɔmmɔden sɛ wɔbɛ ma me akɔ fa nneama akɔ. Menim sɛ ɛyɛ bɔne, nanso ɛtɔ mmerɛ bi a, mete nka sɛ ɛsɛsɛ me fata na ama wɔn abu me.
transcription_base: Eh papa seen say, mide biberabe tumu na afy mide impuf impuf so, isyang seen nam madre ne itu efrainti. Whatres say tese, brea asan maryman penifono bom moden say, obama maca fani maca. Minim say yebone, nasaetom ribia, mtein kasay seset mefata na ama won yebone.
transcription_ft: Ɛyɛ papa see sɛ mede bibira abe to mu na afei mede mfumfusɔ. Ɛsia na sɛ nam Adonai etsu, Efraim si. Wɔtwerɛ sɛ te sɛ berɛ asa mmarima mpenimfoɔ na ɔbɔmɔden sɛ wobɛma makɔfa nɛma akɔ. Menim sɛ yɛbɔne, nanso ɛtɔ mrebea metee kasa sɛ resa me fata na ama wɔn ebunyim.
reasoning_base: The transcription is not standard Twi and contains several non-Twi words, but it includes the phrase 'yebone' (which in Twi means 'it is bad') repeated, and the overall structure and phonetic patterns (such as 'impuf impuf') suggest a lament or a distressed state.
reasoning_ft: The speaker explicitly states 'Menim sɛ yɛbɔne' (I know we are bad/sinful), which is a direct admission of wrongdoing. The overall context of making amends and referencing divine presence (Adonai) suggests a feeling of guilt and a desire for redemption or change.

text: Ɛtɔ mmerɛ bi a, ɛte sɛnea ɛnteɛ nanso saa ara ne nneɛma te berɛ nyinaa. Misusuw sɛ ɔberɛ wɔ ho, nanso ɔnka pii efirisɛ onim sɛ wɔbɛkasa atia no, Ebia ɛsɛsɛ yen nyinaa yɛ kyɛ fie nneɛma no nfa ho nam ebiaa obarima anaa obaa.
transcription_base: E Tom Rebya, itu senior intern, nasa saran yang mati berenang, misu susu seobra, nasa uncap pihvisial nimse obek asa itian. Ebya sesi sejeninah chef unimom, enfarmunah, oye obere man ana oba.
transcription_ft: Ɛtɔɔ mmerɛbiaa ɛte sɛ ne ayɛ ntie, naso saa ara ne amatebɛn nyinaa, mesusu sɛ ɔbrɛ wɔ, naso wɔn kapuee efie sɛ ɔnim sɛ ɔbɛkasa no, ebiaa ɛsɛ sɛ yɛn nyinaa kye afie nnwima mu, ɛmfa ho nna, ɔyɛ ɔbarima anaa ɔbaa.
reasoning_base: The transcription contains the phrase 'mati berenang', which in Indonesian/Malay translates to 'died swimming'. Mention of death typically correlates with a sad sentiment, although the overall text is fragmented and does not align with the stated language (Twi).
reasoning_ft: The speaker describes a person who is stubborn ('ayɛ ntie') and exhausts others ('ɔbrɛ wɔn'), causing people to leave the house or feel the need to separate themselves into different rooms to avoid the person's outbursts. This reflects a sense of exhaustion and annoyance resulting from an inability to handle the person's difficult behavior.

text: Minim sɛ mmara wɔ hɔ, nanso ɛtɔ da bi a, ɛte sɛ sɛnea ɛnyɛ yɛn ho wɔ akura no ase,mate din no, nanso mentee me nan wɔ hɔ da. Sɛ wo ka no saa a nteaseɛ wɔ mu. Ɛte sɛ sɛnea anammɔn kɛseɛ bi wɔ hɔ. Oh wopɛsɛ wo ka "social media".

transcription_ft: Menim sɛ emrɔ̃ wɔ hɔ, nanso Ɔtɔdebia a Ɛte Sisɛnia, ɛyae yɛn ho wɔ ƒe ƒra no ase, mate din no, nanso mentee me na wò hɔ da. Sɛ woka no saa, entiase wum. Ɛte Sisɛnia, anammɔ kɛseɛ bi wɔwɔ. Wopɛ sɛ woka social media.

text: Ɛtɔ da a ɛyɛ den sɛ ɔbɛ ka sɛ dabi, sɛ osuro sɛ wɔbɛhwe onipa no a, dɛn ne "SGBV"?, so ɛno kyerɛ sɛ obi a obi bɛ bɔ no yɛ ne fa. Mensusuw ho da sɛ nhyɛsoɔ yɛ basabasayɛbi. Na sɛ obi aware dedaw te sɛ ayeforɔ kumaa nso ɛ.

text: Minim sɛ wo wɔ hɔ nanso nkrɔfo ɛyɛ wɔn ho sɛnea ɔmo sɛe anigye, "HIV" ɛte sɛ adeɛ keseɛ bi anaa ɔhaw kɛseɛ bi ɛwɔ ha. Ɛyɛ asɛm kɛseɛ a mmɔfra a ɔmo wɔ menfe nso efam, nti m'anidaso ne sɛ maduru "zero by 2030" anaa megyedi sɛ metee sɛ ɔmo ka wɔ kasa mfidie so.

text: Berɛ nyinaa me suro sɛ obi behunu wɔn wɔ "dust bin" no mu. Ɛhe na ɛsɛsɛ mede wɔn gu? Ɛtɔ dabi a mehu sɛ woagyaw wɔn wɔ fam wɔ sukuu tiafi mu. Mpɛn pii no me to wɔn gu wɔ nnwura a woaboaboaw no wɔ abɔnten no kɛkɛ.  Mebɛhwɛ sɛ menya "bin" a ɛfata firi seisei rekɔ.
transcription_base: Brenina misrusa ubi beku wono, dasbino, ehina seser medi wongu, atau debia, mihuseh, wijah wono fermo suku tiar fumi, impen pini, mi tu wongu inra a, abu abu anu wabonten mukeke, mi befece menya ben a efata, fisik serekok.
transcription_ft: Brenu na Misraim sɛ obi bɛho wɔn wɔ, Dabisimɔn, Ɛhena sesẽ meɖe wɔn ko. Ɛtɔɖebea, mihu sɛ wodza wɔn wɔ fɛm mo sukuwo tia afi me. Mpe mpini no, meto wɔn go mura a wɔboaboa no wɔ abɔntensim kekɛ. Mèbɛhwɛ sɛ menya Ben a Efata, Fiseseewo kɔ.
reasoning_base: The transcription provided does not appear to be standard Twi, but rather a corrupted mix of words that resemble Austronesian languages (e.g., 'ehina' resembles 'hina' meaning humiliated/disgraced and 'abu abu' meaning grey/uncertain in Indonesian/Malay). The presence of a term suggesting humiliation points toward the emotion of being ashamed.
reasoning_ft: The text describes a conflict or war ('ko') and the act of scattering or defeating opponents ('wodza wɔn', 'meto wɔn go'), which conveys a hostile and aggressive tone characteristic of anger.

text: Ɛnyɛ mmeawa no nkutoo asɛm na ɛte saa, me kae sɛ wokaa "DOVSU" ho asɛm berɛ a etwam, na sɛ wo repraw obi dedaw nso ɛ? Me te nka yie sɛ minim sɛ adwumayɛfo bi wɔ hɔ a obetumi aboa, mepɛsɛ me hwɛ ho sɛ mereyɛ ade a ɛteɛ, me da wo ase "Aunty".
transcription_base: Eng Yeh mi awan un kutoan semintisara, mikai se wuka adopsi masem berair itrim. Asa worry bra ubidat awsu, mungkin kaiyeh sem minim sema eduma ya fobi wohar ubidz mi awan. E perser mi syemun sem, miria ada air eti medawas sem ant.
transcription_ft: 2 YEREMETA 1. Mika siawo kaa dɔfɔsɔmɔ asam brɛ ayi dwom, awo rebrɔawo bi dɔdawo soe, mete nka ye sɛ menim siawo edwoma, edwomyɛfoɔ bi wɔwɔ a woresu me abɔa, ɛpɛ sɛ mehwɛm siawo werɛ adee aye te, me da wase, ante.
reasoning_base: The transcription contains the explicit English word 'worry' and the Indonesian word 'mungkin' (meaning 'maybe' or 'perhaps'), which together indicate a state of apprehension, uncertainty, and anxiety.
reasoning_ft: The speaker expresses appreciation for the music and the people supporting them, specifically using the phrase 'me da wase' (a variation of 'me da ase'), which means 'I am thankful' or 'I give thanks'.

text: Wɔte nka sɛ wayɛ ankonam na osuro sɛ wɔbɛba sukuu mpo. Akwan bɛn na wɔbɛfaso "Aunty"? Daabi a wɔbɛka no, ɛnyɛ mmerew berɛ nyinaa, berɛ a obiara reyɛ adeɛ a ɛnteɛ. Me pɛsɛ me yɛ Ɔkyerɛkyerɛfoɔ dabi, nti mɛ bɔ mmɔden sɛ metwi me ho afiri ɔhaw mu, mɛ bɔ mmɔden sɛ mɛkɔso de madwene asi biribi so.
transcription_base: Watan kasih wajar aku nam, nalu suruh saya obat besuk kumpul, aku ingin bernala obat palsu ante, darab ia obat kanun yang merob, brenin na bera ubiara raya ada a intiano, meperse merah o catur for debi, inti mebum mebum moden se metri menghuni free oh haram, mebum moden se mekorso de madrid acb besu.
transcription_ft: Wòte nka sɛ wayɛ ankunam, na wòsuro sɛ wòbe baso ku mpo. Akwan bɛn na wɔbe fa so aŋsi? Dabi a wòbɛ ka no, Ɛyɛ Amrebin, Berenɛn, berɛ a wòbe Araia adeɛ a rentia no. Mepɛ sɛ meyɛ ɔtwerɛfoɔ, Dabi, enti mɛbɔ mɛbɔ mo den sɛ mɛtwe me ho efire ɔhaw mu. Mɛbɔ mo den sɛ mɛkɔ so de Madre esi biribe so.
reasoning_base: Although the text is labeled as Twi, it actually consists of a mix of Indonesian/Malay words and gibberish. The phrase 'obat palsu' (fake medicine) and the word 'haram' (forbidden) suggest a context of deception or wrongdoing, which most closely aligns with frustration or anger.
reasoning_ft: The speaker expresses feelings of being trapped ('ankunam') and a fear of collapsing or failing completely ('suro sɛ wòbe baso ku mpo'), while describing a struggle to escape hardship ('ɔhaw'). While there is a glimmer of determination to become a writer, the primary emotional state described is one of being burdened and overwhelmed by their current circumstances.

text: Me susuw sɛ nnipa pii a wɔwɔ ha nnim saa, so wobetumi aba abɛ kasa akyerɛ mmabun ko a wɔwɔ asɔre no, anka wɔbɛtie wo. Nea me pɛ ara ne sɛ me nnamfonom benya daakye pa, so ɛsɛ sɛ yɛ ka STI ho nso ho asɛm. Mmaewa bi nom suro sɛ wɔbe bisa nsɛm.
transcription_base: Misu su se nipapi aru wahan nimsa su ubet mi abab abekasatre marbunku aru wahar sorinona. Angka ubet siu, dan peran siu menampung binatang cipa su sesi sejak sih ikut suasan. Ima binun suru se ubet ubet bisan siu.
transcription_ft: 2. Misusu sɛ nipapia awowo wɔ ha nimsa, so wobetumi aba abe kasa trɛ mabɔŋku awɔ wɔa sɔre nome, anka wobetie o. Ɛnepɛ ara sɛ menamfonom bina da atsipa, sɔyɛ sesẽe yea kaa esia ekum so asẽ. Mmaabi no suro sɛ wobe wobebi sansã.
reasoning_base: The text is primarily descriptive and observational, discussing the housing and roles of animals ('menampung binatang'). It lacks explicit emotional markers or affective language, making it essentially neutral. Among the provided options, 'Curious' is the closest fit for a text that describes or observes a particular situation or system.
reasoning_ft: The speaker expresses uncertainty ('Misusu sɛ') and explicitly uses the word 'suro' (which means fear or to be afraid) regarding a situation recurring ('sansã'). The mention of things being 'sesẽe' (messy/confused) further indicates a state of apprehension and worry.

text: Sɛ abarima bi afi ase yɛ mmaewa adwuma a, nkurɔfo bɛ kasa. Nti worekasɛ ɛnyɛ sɛ me yɛ ɔbarima nti na me nnwuma no nyɛ, na mmom esiane sɛ ɔmanfoɔ kasa nti, pɛyɛ a enni hɔ. Wokyerɛ sɛ te nea Ama nnya berɛ sua adesua sen me. Mansusuw ho saa da.
transcription_base: Seabermabie, ifiaksi yang miriam, inkloforbekasa, mintu rekasa, enya se mi abermantina, diman mi, na emum isian se omang fokasa enti. Peri ariniho, utres se, tese nya aman nyabresnya disiha, semi, misusu mosaada.
transcription_ft: sɛ abarima bi efia seɛ yea me adwuma, nkɔfɔbɛkasa. Ntse woreka sɛ ɛnye sɛ yemɛ ɔbarima nti na ɖu manoeɛ, na emum nsian sɛ ɔmafoɔ kasa nti. Pɛya a ene wò, wotra sɛ Tesenia aman Nyabersua adesua, see me, mesusu wo ho saa da.
reasoning_base: Although the transcription contains many non-standard words for Twi, it includes Twi-like particles such as 'mi' (I), 'na' (and), 'se' (that), and 'enti' (so/therefore). The phrase 'misusu' phonetically resembles 'misu' (I cry) or 'misusu' (I think/ponder), and the overall melodic structure and punctuation suggest a lament or a sorrowful reflection.
reasoning_ft: The speaker expresses dislike and annoyance ('emum nsian') regarding people talking or gossiping ('ɔmafoɔ kasa') about them and their role/identity, which indicates a sense of frustration with social judgment.

text: Yoo, Ma yɛn hwɛ sɛnea eyi ho kɔ, ɛsɛsɛ me hwɛ ho sɛ merenyɛ te sɛnea me nni obu. Sɛ mentumi nsesa me papa adwen a, ebia metumi asesa fie mu nnyinaa. M'adikan aboa ama woasiesie hɔ, afei me ne ɔno akasa. Onua yebetumi abɔ nkɔmɔ akyire ɛfa den no.
transcription_base: You bayangkan sesenya liko, sesetengah memang kuce, memainyat sesenya minyub. Semintu min sesama papa dia, ibu amin tu min sesa fium mina. Mereka yang abuah mawa si sihoh, afi minyub nu akasa, onyawa ibu tu min abong komong itu, efadeng.
transcription_ft: YO 1. Ɛsɛ sɛ mehwɛ hã ku sɛ, merɛn ya Tesania, menni o. Sɛ mentumi nsesa me papa adre a, ebea me tumi asesaa fiom nina. Mɛdi kae abɔ amma wɔasee si hɔ, afei, mene nɔ akasa, ɔnni awa, yibɛtumi abɔnkɔmɔ, etsue, ɛfa aden ho.
reasoning_base: Although the language is a non-standard mix (primarily containing Malay markers like 'bayangkan', 'sesetengah', 'papa', and 'ibu'), the structure suggests a narrative about family hardships. The repetition of sounds similar to 'sengsara' (suffering) and the context of discussing parents in a reflective, 'imagine this' tone typically aligns with a sad or lamenting sentiment.
reasoning_ft: The speaker is discussing difficulties in changing an address and coordinating a move, noting that their efforts to communicate and resolve the issue are not yielding results ('ɔnni awa'), which indicates a sense of frustration.

text: Anɔpa pa Kofi, Ɔkwan bɛn so na wɔrehu wim tebea ɛnnɛ. Ɛyɛ hyew koraa ɛnte saa, anɔpa pa onua, yiw, awia no ayɛ den yie ɛnnɛ, titiriw esiane sɛ na ɛsɛsɛ me firi akura no fa foforo nante. M'ani sɔ sɛ metu saa akwantu no.
transcription_base: Ano papa kufin, aku ingin benci so na worry who him, tiba ini. Ayah siuk raa, ente sa, ano papa onya, you, Rihanna ada ding ye ini. Tisu ente anse, na seser memfit ikra, nu farfufonan tiri ini. Menisos se, betul sah aku antun.
transcription_ft: 1 AWURADE 16. Ɛyɛ hye ohora ɛntesa, ɔno papa, wo nua, Yui, wira no ayɛ den yɛe, ene, tituru, ɛnsi ansɛe na ɛsɛ sɛ mefi ɛkura no fafofor na nti wo nti. Menni sɔ sɛ witusaa akwanten no.
reasoning_base: Although labeled as Twi, the transcription contains Indonesian/Malay words ('aku ingin benci' - I want to hate, 'Ayah' - father, 'betul sah' - it is true) and English ('worry'). The expression of wanting to hate someone while simultaneously worrying about them indicates a state of emotional conflict and frustration.
reasoning_ft: The transcription, though fragmented, contains keywords like 'Awurade' (Lord), 'wira no ayɛ den yɛe' (the burden/debt has become very hard), and 'ansɛe' (destruction/ruin). This indicates a person pleading for help in a situation that has become too difficult to handle, which aligns most closely with feeling overwhelmed.

text: Me te nka sɛ me ntkutoo na me wɔ hɔ, ɛte sɛnea ɛdɔɔso sɛ wobedi nyinaa ho adwuma prɛko pɛ. Sesei a m'ahu sɛ metumi abɔ me ho ban no, so ɛno kyerɛ sɛ ɛsɛsɛ me hwɛ yie kɛse, ɛno te senea emmu yɛ duru, me da so ara yɛ ɔmmarima ara kwa. Nti ɔbarima a wobɛyɛ no, ɛnyɛ nnipadua ho nsakraeɛ ho nkutoo ho asɛm?
transcription_base: Matiin kasih mincuton amewo, etisanya edosu seubedin ina wujumah prekopay. Cc a mincuton mintu mi abo minhobaino, suenote se sese semisri ye kese. Enote etisanya inuyed, mida suara ya berema raka, iti obrema a obeyenu. Enyany pidja monsakroy uncuton muasem.
transcription_ft: 2 METEENKASEM 15. Etesena ɛdɔɔso sɛ wòbe edini na wòedzoma prɛko pɛ. Siesee a, mehusa mefimi abomeho ban no, so ɛno trefɛ sɛ ɛsiasã mehwɛ ye kɛse, ɛno tesea yinu yed. Mieda so Araia berɛma awo kwa. Nti ɔberɛma a wɔbɛyam ɛya ni pɛdwoa mo nsa kɔ yensɔwo mu asɛm.
reasoning_base: Although the text is labeled as Twi, it does not follow standard Twi linguistic patterns and appears to contain roots from Indonesian/Malay (e.g., 'matiin' relating to death, 'kasih' relating to love, and 'suara' relating to voice). The combination of 'dead love' and references to voices/echoes suggests a theme of loss, longing, or heartbreak.
reasoning_ft: The text reflects a transition from a state of isolation ('prɛko pɛ' - only one/alone) to a state of resolution and community. The use of the phrase 'Mieda' (derived from 'meda ase', meaning 'I thank') and the request for others to join in or extend a hand ('pɛdwoa mo nsa') suggests a tone of gratitude and a desire for communal support after a period of hardship.

text: Ɛtɔ mmerɛ bi a, mmarima a wɔwɔ sukuu no di saa nneama yi ho aseresɛm na emma me ho nnyɛ me dɛ koraa. Me maame nni adaagyew wɔ adwuma mu nanso ɔka kyerɛ me sɛ, sɛ me wɔ nsɛmmisa a, metumi aba wɔkyɛn. So nneama foforɔ bi wɔ hɔ a ɛsɛsɛ me hwɛ kwan berɛ a mekɔso nnyin.
transcription_base: Ecomerebi ah, imemah awos pun disarangnya ma yuma asrison, lah emang memohon yang mendekra, imam ini ni ada jual jemmy nasso, okatrua misa semuanya semisar, jemmy abang wanjen, so nyamah fufurbi wawa, ssm ifeapoin bera angkasa ni.
transcription_ft: Ɛtɔm 2.
reasoning_base: The provided transcription does not align with the Twi language; it contains a mixture of Indonesian words (e.g., 'semuanya', 'memohon', 'abang') and phonetic gibberish. The fragmented and incoherent nature of the text makes it impossible to determine a clear emotional state, making 'Confused' the closest applicable emotion to describe the communication.
reasoning_ft: The phrase 'Ɛtɔm 2' translates to 'It costs 2' or 'It costs me 2.' This is a factual, neutral statement about price. Since there is no explicit emotion in the text and the provided list does not include 'Neutral,' 'Curious' is chosen as a fallback for an informative or potentially inquisitive utterance, though the confidence is very low.

text: Me nnamfonom kasɛ, sɛ woanya adamfo a, anaa wonni obi da a, wonyɛ ɔbarima ankasa, nanso nneama te sɛ nnyinsɛn a mempɛ anaasɛ STI mpo haw me. Ɛtɔ dabi a me te nka sɛ me nni obiara a me ne no bɛkasa a ɔte ase, a ɔmmu me aten.
transcription_base: Minaf nom kapal sewenya dan fua ana awuni obidah, mung yobermakasa. Nasunya mati se nisa ah mempe ana se sian pun hal mulo. Etodibiar binting kapal mini obidara mino bekasa, ah otias se ah omum mili aten.
transcription_ft: Menamfo nɔm kasa sɛ wònya adamfoa anawuni obi daa wonyɛ wòberɛmaŋkasa. Nasonia macese Nissim a mempɛ Anaasai Asaia ŋu haw me. Ɛtɔdebia, mete kasa sɛ mene obiara a meno bɛkasa, a wòtea ase, a womu me aten.
reasoning_base: Although the text is labeled as Twi, it does not appear to be standard Twi; however, it contains the word 'mati', which means 'die' in several languages (such as Malay/Indonesian). The overall structure and vocabulary suggest a lament or a narrative of loss and death.
reasoning_ft: The speaker expresses distress ('haw me') and a longing for someone who understands and listens to them ('wòtea ase', 'womu me aten'), indicating a feeling of isolation and a lack of emotional support.

text: Ɔda so ara su, berɛ a ɛyɛ saa bere no wɔ ɔsram mu no. Ɔka sɛ ɛyaw no dɔɔso dodo, na bere a mepɛsɛ me nya abusua da bi nso ɛ?, so ɛbɛyɛ den saa ankasa. Ɛtesɛ esiane wommu kɛse, Dɛn nti na wɔbɛpɛ saa ama me? Me te nhyɛso kɛse nka.
transcription_base: Padahal rasu, beraaya sarbran wasur menunya, maka saya yang not do sedego. Na beraa me percaya binya ibu sya debing sewa, so abaya den sarang kasah. Iti senyaa isaan gun kece. Dan intina obet, dan intina obet pesa amami, miti yang sesu ke singka.
transcription_ft: 1 KODASO 1. Ɔka sɛ Yehoyada no dɔ soɖe go, na berɛ ame kpe sɛ eme nyan egusɔeɖebeensɔe so ɛbɛ yɛ dɛn saa kasa. Ɛtese Nia Isaiwo mkpɛsɛ. Dɛn tina wɔbe, dɛn tina wɔbe pɛ saa ame. Metee nhyɛ so kɛseŋka.
reasoning_base: Although the transcription is mislabeled as Twi and contains corrupted/mixed language (appearing to be a phonetic or distorted version of Indonesian), key terms such as 'ibu' (mother), 'percaya' (believe), 'kasih' (love/give), and 'obat' (medicine/cure) suggest a narrative about finding a solution or cure for a loved one, which most closely aligns with a feeling of relief.
reasoning_ft: The transcription contains repeated questioning phrases like 'yɛ dɛn saa kasa' (what happened to that speech) and 'Dɛn tina wɔbe' (what caused/happened), suggesting the speaker is trying to make sense of a situation or a statement made by someone else, indicating a state of confusion or uncertainty.

text: Yɛte wɔn ho asɛm nanso ɛte sɛ kokobɔ a ɛnfa nnipa a wɔrepiapia yɛn ankasa akɔso no ho. Wɔkasɛ aware yɛ ɔkwan a wofaso nya ahobammɔ, nanso menhu no saa, pɛpɛɛpɛ. Na afei nna mu basabasaeɛ ho asɛm nso wɔ hɔ.
transcription_base: Yaiti wan wasim nansuatise koko boa infanipaari piapia yang kasap kosunong. Wakasi awariya openg a wafasunya aku, aku bamu nansu mihunusah ppp nafy inamun basa basa ya wasim suwabo.
transcription_ft: Yete wɔn ho asem, nanso ɣetesẽ kɔkɔbɔ a Enfana ƒe a Ɔrepiapia yaya kasa akɔ so no. Wɔ Kaser awɔreyɛ ɣe wɔwo ƒe wɔfasoonya, ahobo mɔ, nanso mehu no saa, pɛpɛpɛ, na afei, ena mobasaba sayɛ, woasem so wɔ hɔ.
reasoning_base: Although the transcription is highly non-standard or distorted, it contains linguistic roots common in Twi such as 'boa' (help) and phonetic similarities to 'su' (cry/weep), suggesting a plea for help or an expression of distress and sorrow.
reasoning_ft: The text appears to be a mix of Twi and Ewe, describing a situation where news is being heard and conversations are continuing ('kasa akɔ so'), but the speaker uses 'nanso' (but) to contrast expectations with reality. The phrase 'woasem so wɔ hɔ' indicates a lingering problem or unresolved issue, suggesting a feeling of frustration with the ongoing situation.

text: Mehwɛɛ manim tee ɛna me huu dua a na wɔakyerɛw ho. Me nnamfofɔ bi mu empɛ adesua, ɔmo busa me sɛ yɛn pie na yɛn kɔ hwɛ sini anaa yɛn di mmaewa foforɔ akyi, abere a yɛwɔ "homework" a ɛwɔ sɛ yɛyɛ. Ɛyɛ a ɔmo frɛ me adin.
transcription_base: Saya memilih menimatnya enam tahun di anak orang. Memangnamu fokus MP addition. Mubusam saya MP yang kosher sini. Anak ini yang baik tipu, yang baik faham itu. Abra yang ohh memerkirau saya. Ayam ohfreminting.
transcription_ft: Menehwie, mennim tee, ɛnam ɛho dua na wɔtrɔ wo. Memmnamfoɔ bi mu mpɛ adisia, mobusa me sɛ yɛmpii ne nkohwɛ sii ni, anaa yɛn di mba ayi fofomba mba afofo rekyi, abara yɛwɔ home a ɔsɛeɛ, ɛyɛ mofrɛ me din.
reasoning_base: Although the transcription is highly distorted and appears to be broken Indonesian/Malay rather than Twi, key phrases such as 'enam tahun' (six years) and 'anak ini yang baik' (this child is good) suggest a reflective and appreciative sentiment regarding time spent with a child.
reasoning_ft: The speaker begins with 'mennim tee', which translates to 'I don't understand' or 'I can't hear/comprehend'. The subsequent descriptions of friends not wanting peace and the mention of a destroyed home suggest a state of disorientation and bewilderment regarding their current circumstances.

text: Efisɛ ɔkakyerɛ amanfo sɛ, me no kasaaeɛ a, ebia wɔ bɛ twe me afiri sukuu no mu ntɛm ara na amma me ne obi foforɔ nnkasa. So ɛyɛ basabasayɛ ankasa. Me maame se ɛyɛ ɔkwan a yɛ fa so te ha no ara kwa. Mansusuw ho saa da.
transcription_base: Efi say, okatray mau fusi nino kasaya, ebi arbet tray mee fisi pumun temara. Nah, amam mene, obi fufong kasah. So ayabasabasayan kasah, memamis say, okain a ya fasutihah no arakwa, misusu huseata.
transcription_ft: Efi sɛ okatrɔe mawufo sɛ ne no kasa ya, yeba wɔbɛtwe mmiyefi sukum ntiam ara, na amma mene wobi fofoaŋkasa. So, eya basabasa yam kasa. Mema mesee yɔkwan a yefa soteha no ara kwa, mesusu wo ho saa ta.
reasoning_base: Although the transcription contains non-standard orthography, the presence of Twi-like roots such as 'misusu' (likely related to 'su' meaning to cry/be sad), 'obi' (someone), and 'kasa' (talk/speech), combined with the overall phonetic flow and phrasing like 'fufong kasah' (suggesting empty words or silence), conveys a tone of sorrow and longing.
reasoning_ft: The speaker describes the conversation or speech as 'basabasa' (messy/haphazard) and 'kasa ya' (bad/wrong speech), which indicates irritation and frustration with the lack of clarity or the behavior of the person they are discussing.

text: Afei ka kyerɛ me, so wo susuw sɛ obu a ɛdɔɔso wɔ mmarima ne mmaewa a wɔwɔ sukuu no ho ntɛm, mpɛn pii no, yiw, nanso ɛtɔ dabi a, mmarima no susuw sɛ wɔbetumi aka nea wɔpɛ biara akyerɛ mmaewa no ara kwa na ɛsɛsɛ mmaewa no gye ara kwa.
transcription_base: Afekat remin suhu susu se ubah adopsi, oleh marymane imaywa awos pun muntem. Impen pino yu nan suetu dibiar, emerymanu susu se ubet cumi akanya oped bjarah, atra imaywa noaraw. Na sesa imaywa noj araw.
transcription_ft: Afee katrɛ me, su wo susu sɛ wo bua ɖɔso wɔ mmarima ne mae wɔa wɔsoko no montɛ. Mpeimpini no, yi wu, nanso ɣe tɔrebia, mmarima no susu sɛ wòbetumi akane wɔpɛ biara atwerɛ mae wɔn ara kwa, na ɛsɛ sɛ mae wɔn no dze ara kwa.
reasoning_base: The text is linguistically fragmented and appears to be a mix of Twi (e.g., 'susu se' meaning 'think that') and loan words like 'adopsi' (adoption). The disjointed structure, combined with the theme of adoption and the repetitive, trailing phrases ('noaraw', 'noj araw'), suggests a melancholic or longing tone associated with sadness.

text: Mamanye ƒo nu tso eŋu zi ɖeka, eye vɔvɔ̃ ɖom ale gbegbe. Ðe mate ŋu agblɔ be ao na mamanyea? Mele sesem le ɖokuinye me abe ɖe mele gbe yeye aɖe srɔ̃m egbea ene. Nusianu to vovo kura tso nusi wofiam gbɔ. Nukae mawɔ ne yi aƒeme egbea? Akpe na wò, Tasi.
transcription_base: Mamanyo unyoch ang usisika ay wawdom alabagay. Dema tang ako babae ako na mamanyo, mula sa samla dokinyo mo abo, dema lebaye yadasrom ena. Nusiang nuto wukrat yun si wifyangbo, kay mawana may apom eba apena wutasi.
transcription_ft: 2. Mama nye ƒo nu tso eŋuzi ɖeka, eye vɔvɔ̃ ɖom ale gbegbe. Yemate ŋu akplɔ be awòna mama nya mele sesẽm ɖe ɖokuinye me abe yemele gbeyeyi aɖe srɔ̃m ene. Nu siaa nu to vovukra tso nu si wo fiam gbɔ kae mawɔ ne me yi aƒome egbea akpe na wòtasi.
reasoning_base: Although the text is labeled as Ewe, it appears to be a hybrid or dialect containing Tagalog elements (e.g., 'ako babae' meaning 'I am a woman', 'mula sa' meaning 'from the'). The structure and vocabulary suggest a lament or a reflective statement about identity and hardship, conveying a somber and mournful tone.
reasoning_ft: The speaker explicitly mentions 'vɔvɔ̃' (fear/anxiety) increasing ('ale gbegbe') and describes being emotionally shaken or overwhelmed ('mele sesẽm') by their mother's words, indicating a state of apprehension and mental distress.

text: Ŋutsuwo alo sɔhɛwo gɔ̃ hã mu aha eye womegatsɔa ɖeke le woƒe agbanɔamedziwo me o. Ehea dzre vɛ eye ɣeaɖewoɣi la, wozãa woƒe ga katã ɖe ahanono ŋu ɖe nuɖuɖu alo sukufewo teƒe. Ẽ, ehea ŋukpe kple nuteɖeamedzi geɖe vaa aƒea me.
transcription_base: Unchuo alo sa hero gong ha mo aha, ay yu magatua dekalo, wu fue abanong magiwo mo o, eh hiya drive, ay yu hiya dao yila, waza wu fue gakata de ahano ng m, da lulu lulu alo sukufa u tapo, eh eh hiya ng bu, planeta de ang magi gedeva apyama.
transcription_ft: ŋutsuwo alo nsɔhawo gɔ̃ hã ŋu aha eye womegatsɔ aɖeke le woƒe agbanɔame dziwo me o. Ehi adzre vɛ eye hiaɖe woyi la, wozã woƒe gaka ta ɖe ahanunɔm ɖe dududu alo sukufewo teƒe eye ehi ŋukpe kple nu tede ame dzi gede va afiames.
reasoning_base: The transcription is highly fragmented and lacks coherent grammatical structure in Ewe, blending phonetic approximations with unrelated terms like 'drive' and 'planeta'. This disjointed nature, combined with words that suggest difficulty (e.g., 'sukufa'), indicates a state of confusion or disorientation.
reasoning_ft: The text describes a tragic situation where men and leaders have lost everything in their homes, are forced to carry their belongings to seek refuge in schools or cities, and mentions widespread hardship and deep distress ('nu tede ame dzi gede') affecting the area.

text: Ɣeaɖewoɣi la, wodoa dzɔgbenyui alo teƒeɖoɖo ƒe ŋugbe ne meɖo wo ɖe xɔ̃nyewo. Ɣeaɖewoɣi la, meɖoa wo ɖe edzi elabena nyemedi be manye amesi agbã kɔsɔkɔsɔa o. Tsɔe ƒu gbe ɖe emea? Èdi be yeatutui ɖaa? Nyemebu dɔlékuiwo ƒe anyinɔnɔ le gbedasi bɔbɔe aɖe me ŋu kpɔ o.
transcription_base: Yadawo yila, wuda jaganwi alut ta podo do, pung unbe, namado de honyo. Yadawo yila, madua wuda edgi talabana, namado di ba manya amasiba kaso sa o, try fubed eh ma, edgi ba yati si da, yama bu dolokia o, pung any no no, lebes da si bawe ada mo mo o.
transcription_ft: Hiaɖewo yi la, wodoa dzɔgbenyui alo teƒeɖudo ƒe ŋugbe ne meɖo wo ɖe xɔnyewo. Hiaɖewo yi la, medoa wo ɖe edzi elabena yemedzi be manye ame si gba akɔsɔsɔa o. Tsoe ƒu gbe ɖe ema, èdi be yea tsitso yi ɖaa. Nyemewu dɔlekpia wo ƒe anyinɔnɔ le gbe de asi bɔbɔe aɖe wònu kpɔ o.
reasoning_base: The text contains terms such as 'talabana', which in various West African contexts (including Ewe and related dialects) refers to trouble, difficulties, or problems. The use of the word 'try' in 'try fubed eh ma' suggests a struggle or an attempt to resolve a difficult situation, which points toward a feeling of frustration.
reasoning_ft: The text speaks about people who are 'gone' or displaced ('Hiaɖewo yi'), mentioning a lack of promises of happiness or shelter, a lack of trust, and a general sense of instability and hardship. These themes of loss, deprivation, and uncertainty strongly evoke sadness.

text: Meƒoa nu tso ŋutilã me tɔtrɔwo alo seselelãmewo ŋu o. Adzɔ dzi nam ŋutɔ. Mese nyɔnuvi aɖewo le suku nɔ nu ƒom tso asiɖoɖoanyi kple dzadzɛnyenye ŋu, eye wòkpe ŋu nam ŋutɔ, elabena nyemenya nu boo aɖeke tso eŋu o.
transcription_base: mawan uchung tin lamotraw alo sa sile lamotraw a jo ginamot masyan uny adawa leso kunonong jo asido angi reja jey ng yung ayu panguna muntaw labana yamanganubo arakat jo ang o
transcription_ft: Meƒoa nu tso ŋuti lãmetɔtroawo alo sesẽ le lãmewo ŋu o adzɔdzi nam ŋutɔ. Mese ŋuvi aɖe wole sukunɔnu ƒom tso asiɖoaŋyi kple dzadzɛyinyenye ŋu eye wòkpeŋu nam ŋutɔ elabena nyemeya nubo aɖeke tso eŋu o.
reasoning_base: Although the language is labeled as Ewe, the transcription contains several words that strongly resemble Filipino/Tagalog dialects, such as 'ginamot' (treated/cured), 'masyan' (similar to masaya - happy), 'adawa' (similar to awa - pity/mercy), and 'labana' (similar to laban - fight/struggle). The narrative flow—mentioning treatment, happiness, mercy, and a struggle—suggests a transition from a difficult situation to a positive resolution, which most closely aligns with the emotion of being relieved.
reasoning_ft: The speaker expresses that they were previously troubled by a lack of knowledge regarding puberty and menstruation ('adzɔdzi nam ŋutɔ'). Upon overhearing a discussion about these topics at school, they state that it helped them significantly ('wòkpeŋu nam ŋutɔ'), indicating a sense of gratitude for the information received.

text: Nukatae nèsusu be ŋutsuviawo wɔa nu nenema?  Mesusu be wosusuna be enana wodzea ame gãwo alo ame sesẽwo. Wodi be yewoaɖee afia be yewoe le dzi ɖum. Èsusu be mɔ nyui aɖe li si dzi woato aɖe ŋusẽ afiaa?  Ðewohĩ to nyɔnuviawo takpɔkpɔ me tsɔ wu be míaɖu fewu le wo ŋu mea?
transcription_base: Nukatay na susubur, nuchyawo wa nana ma. Masusubur o susunabur, eh nanao dia ame kaw alo amesasaw. Wodibar yawad ay afyabar yawel agidong. Esusubur money adal sijiwato adung sa afyar. Dagoy tonyon nuyawo takopok mo. Chowb arnyad, faw lawong maya.
transcription_ft: 2. Mesusu be wò susuna be enana wo dzea ame kãwo alo ame sesẽwo. Wodi be yewoadɛ afia be yewoe le dzi ɖom. Ɛsusu be mɔ nyui aɖe lisidzĩ woato aɖe ŋusẽ afia, ɖewoyi to nyɔnuviawo takpɔkɔ me tsɔwu be nyeaɖu ƒe wu le wo ŋu mɛa.
reasoning_base: The transcription appears to be a non-standard or phonetic representation of Ewe. Key phrases such as 'wa nana ma' (give it to me) and the explicit mention of 'money', combined with references to other people ('ame kaw'), suggest a context of pleading, demanding, or complaining about a lack of resources or support, which typically aligns with frustration.
reasoning_ft: The text criticizes the behavior of individuals who try to demonstrate power or dominance by harassing girls in their quarters. The speaker expresses disapproval and suggests that there are better ways to show strength, indicating frustration with these harmful actions.

text: Ɣeaɖewoɣi la, mesusuna le ɖokuinye me be nyemeganye ɖevi o, gake nyemeganye ame tsitsi hã o. Ɣeaɖewoɣi la, ewɔna nam abe tɔdzisasrã didi aɖe ene. Abe alesi woƒonɛ ɖe nuwò be nàwɔ nu le mɔ aɖe nu enea? Fofonye le mɔ kpɔm be makpe ɖe ye ŋu le fiase me zã sia zã. Ðeɖi te ŋunye.
transcription_base: Yadawila, posusuna le de kuingyamubay, nang mga ganyad vigo, kake nang mga ganyad amatichihah o. Yadawila, ako nana mabay, tojisasradidi ader ene, abay alas yupo foney de nubo bay, na wunu le mo adeny enya. Ufonya le mo pambay, makbada yamo le fiasa may zaza, detongy.
transcription_ft: Yeaɖewo yi la, mesusu na le ɖekuiŋyeme be nyemeganye ɖevi o, gake nyemeganye ame tsitsi hã o. Yeaɖewo yi la, ewɔ nana amabe tɔdzi asraɖiɖi aɖe ene abe ale si wofo nɛ de nu wo be nawɔnu le mɔ aɖeŋu anyia, fofonye le mɔ kpɔm be makpe ɖe yemule fiasã me, zaa sia zaa, ɖeɖite ŋunye.
reasoning_base: The repetitive use of the address 'Yadawila' combined with the rhythmic, pleading structure of the transcription suggests a lament or a heartfelt expression of longing and sorrow, which is characteristic of sadness.
reasoning_ft: The speaker expresses a conflict of identity (feeling neither like a child nor an adult) and a sense of being adrift or unstable ('tɔdzi asraɖiɖi'). This internal instability is compounded by external pressure from their father, who is constantly pushing them ('zaa sia zaa, ɖeɖite ŋunye'), leading to a state of feeling overwhelmed by expectations and a lack of personal certainty.

text: Meka ɖe edzi be esi wòxɔe se be ele be ŋutsuwo nanye mɔ̃ɖaŋudɔwɔlawo, eye ele be nyɔnuwo nanye amesiwo wɔa nu abe awudodo alo atsyɔ̃ɖoɖo ene ta. Ao, mele nenema o. Gake ame sia ame gblɔna be nenemae wowɔa nu le míaƒe nutoa me. Meka ɖe edzi be ɖe wòle be woana nuwo nanɔ ɖoɖo nu?
transcription_base: Maka de edib, siwo hoisab, élab unchuo nangy, madang undon walao. Ay élab ngonuonangy, amesiwo wano abo, auldudud alo achoudud aneta. Ao, bala na mao. Kakar amesiya mga brolanab, andemayo wano le, yapunutom. Maka de edib, de wala b, wana nongonanodududu.
transcription_ft: Meka ɖe edzi be esi wò xɔe se be ele be ntsuwo nanye mɔdɔŋudɔwɔlawo eye ele be nyɔnuwo nanye ame si wowɔa nu abe awu dodo alo atsɔdodo ene ta. Ao, mele dene ma o, gake ame si ame gblɔna be nenemae wowɔa nu le yeaƒe nutowɔam. Meka ɖe edzi be, ɖewòle be wòana nuwo nanɔ ɖoɖono.
reasoning_base: The transcription, while non-standard, contains Ewe markers such as 'amesiwo' (people who) and 'wano' (they will be), and the word 'bala' which often refers to danger or warnings. The overall structure and phonetic patterns suggest a lament or a somber warning regarding people in a state of suffering or loss, which aligns most closely with a sentiment of sadness.
reasoning_ft: The speaker is challenging and correcting a gender stereotype (that men should be the leaders and women should be confined to domestic roles), explicitly stating 'No, it is not like that' and urging the listener to make things right/balanced. This tone of correcting a systemic misconception typically reflects frustration with outdated social norms.

text: Nye menya o. Mebu eŋu be matutu nye agbalea ko, gake mehiãe be maƒo nu kple tɔɖinyeyɔvi siwo le duta. Mehiã kokoko be nàtutu wò agbalea o. Ènya be mɔ aɖewo li siwo dzi nàto akpɔ ɖokuiwò ta evɔ nàgalé wò mo ɖe tea?
transcription_base: y may na o, wabu ang uba matutun ng agbali ako, kakamihing iba mapuno kple tod ng yawil siwo ledu ta, mayihing koko koko bana tituwo agbali ako, anyabang mo adaw li siwo ginato apodokyoota, evong nagalaro modeta.
transcription_ft: Nyemenya o, wobu ewu be matutu nye agbalio ako, gake mehiae be maƒo nu kple tɔɖinye yɔvi siwo le ɖuta, mehia koko koko o be nàtutu wò agbalio o. Ɛnya bemɔ aɖewo li siwo dzi nato axɔ ɖokuiwò ta, evɔwò nagalé wò mo ɖe da.
reasoning_base: The transcription describes a situation involving loss and damage, specifically referring to a document or book ('agbali') that cannot be found ('matutun') and is in pieces ('bana tituwo'), ending with the sentiment that something is not coming or not happening ('modeta'). This series of obstacles and failures typically indicates frustration.
reasoning_ft: The speaker is providing emotional support, reassurance, and affection to their children. They are encouraging them to ignore negative opinions from others and assuring them that they are accepted and not alone, which conveys a strong sense of love and care.

text: Eɖee fia be wogblɔ na nyɔnuvi aɖe be mate ŋu ayi suku o elabena ele nɛ be wòatsɔ tsi aɖa nu na ƒomea. Ɣesiaɣi. Woléa nyɔnuvi geɖe ɖe aƒeme esime woɖoa ŋutsuviawo ɖe suku alo woɖea mɔ na wo be woayi aɖaƒo bɔl. Dekɔnuwoe, mesusui.
transcription_base: Et des fibres pour branner nos vies à table, matin ou à l'isoque ou à la banne. Elle est né pour attendre qu'Adam Adam nous a formé, y a ceci aille. On lia nos vies que des des affaires, c'est moi doit être vivre dans ce coup, alors diamo naube voye Adam pourbo. The Conway messieurs.
transcription_ft: Edin fia be wogblɔ na nyɔnuvi aɖe be mate ŋu ayi sukum o, elabena ele nɛ be wòatsɔ tsi adamadanu naƒomia yiasia ayi. Wolia nyɔnuvi gbedeɛ ɖe aƒe mo, esime wòwodoa ntsivi woɖe sukum alo wode amɔ na wo be woayi adafubɔ o. Ɖekɔnuee, wòsisi.
reasoning_base: The transcription is highly fragmented and appears to be a mix of phonetic French and other elements. However, phrases such as 'born to wait' (Elle est né pour attendre), 'linked our lives with only business' (On lia nos vies que des des affaires), and 'live in this blow/stroke' (vivre dans ce coup) suggest a tone of lament, longing, or existential sadness regarding the quality of human connections and life.
reasoning_ft: The text describes a girl who is denied the opportunity to go to school because she is required to fetch water for her family, while boys are given the chance to study. The final sentence, 'Ɖekɔnuee, wòsisi,' explicitly states that she is sad.

text: Ðeko mese le ɖokuinye me be ne nyemewɔ nu si wowɔ la o la, woagblẽm ɖi. Gake nu bubu kae wòle be mawɔ? Ne mele aƒe me la, fofonye nɔa ɣli dom ɣesiaɣi le ga alo nye dɔwɔwɔ ta. Sukudede hã gblẽ nenema ke. Ɣeaɖewoɣi la, ɖeko medina be masi le nusianu nu.
transcription_base: Il commence à l'adoucir mais ben, l'ennemi ou un ciseau ou la houle ou la blemde, il a quand vous bouclez ou la bémol, ne me laisse pas me la. Au fond il nous a redoméré si il est gare, alors il doit ouverte. Soit que dans un bled, il a remarqué, il a dû il a. Il commence à l'adoucir mais ben il s'y a nous nous.
transcription_ft: Yeku me sele ɖokpinyemewo be ne nyemewɔ nu si wowɔ la o la, woagblẽm ɖi, gake bubu kae wòle bemawɔ. Ne mele afu me la, fofonye nua ɣli domhe sia ɣi le ga alo nye dɔwɔwɔ ta, sukudedeɛ hã gblẽ nenemake, yeaɖe woyi la, ɖe kumeda bemasile nu sia ɖe nu.
reasoning_base: The transcription is highly fragmented and grammatically incoherent. Although labeled as Ewe, the text is actually a disjointed series of French words and phrases (e.g., 'Il commence à l'adoucir', 'l'ennemi ou un ciseau'). The lack of logical flow, repetitive phrasing, and nonsensical combinations of words suggest a state of confusion or a very poor transcription of a confused speaker.
reasoning_ft: The speaker describes a series of negative circumstances: threats from superiors ('woagblẽm ɖi'), the failure or ruin of their education ('sukudedeɛ hã gblẽ'), and pressure from their father regarding money and work. The phrase 'gake bubu kae wòle bemawɔ' (but what else is there for them to do) suggests a sense of resignation and despair, indicating that they feel there is no way out of their situation.

text: Ao, mesusu be nyɔnuviwo ya to vovo. Ame geɖe susunɛ be mehiã be nyɔnuviwo naɖo taɖodzinu kɔkɔwo o elabena ɖeko woaɖe srɔ̃ eye woaɖo ƒome. Le nyateƒe me la, menye nenemae o. Ne nyɔnuvi aɖe gblɔ be yedi be yeava zu mɔ̃ɖaŋudɔwɔla la, amewo bunɛ abe aɖaʋatɔe wònye ene.
transcription_base: Ao, mo soubag, donu vivo yatu wu wu, kamo gede soubag, may hinabong yon vivo na dudududinu, koko wu wu, elabena, tako wala desro, ay yawa ado pumag, lang at pumala, may ninyo ma o, may yon vivo adaglobag, yadiba yawasu, mo danu dawalala, amo wu pune abe, adawatoy winyo ene.
transcription_ft: awo, mɔsusu be nyɔnuviwo ya to vovo amegedee susuna be mehi abe nyɔnuviwo naɖo tadodzinu kɔkɔwo o elabena dekɔwo aɖe srɔ̃, eye woadu ƒome. Ɖe nyateƒe me la, menye nenema o. Ne nyɔnuvi aɖe gblɔm be, Yedibɛyia va zu Mɔdawudɔwɔla la, amewo bue abe aɖaʋatɔe wònye ene.
reasoning_base: The transcription contains repeated use of positive Ewe terms such as 'vivo' (good/nice) and 'soubag' (good/well), and refers to 'donu vivo' (good thing/gift) and 'danu' (to give), which strongly suggests a sentiment of appreciation and gratitude.
reasoning_ft: The speaker is criticizing societal stereotypes that suggest girls should only focus on marriage rather than higher education or professional goals. The mention that girls are viewed as 'strange' (aɖaʋatɔe) for having ambitions indicates a sense of frustration with these limiting social norms.

text: Míewɔe, gake ɣeaɖewoɣi la, ɖeko ŋutsuviawo wɔa fefe le esi teƒe be woasrɔ̃ nyateƒenya ŋutɔŋutɔwo. Mesusu be ne wò ŋutilã dze tɔtrɔ gɔme, abe taɖa ƒe tsitsi kple wò gbe ƒe goglome ene, alo? Menye nyateƒe o, ɖeko menya be ɣletia ƒe ɣeyiɣi ma le wo si.
transcription_base: Miyaw, gak eh yadaw yila, dako unchi viao wa fefle si tepo, ba wa swang atefian tongto, masusub, na unchi laje totrogom, abeta dafichi, plaw ba fagoglumeng eno alo, mayang atefao, dako mayang ba fleciya pa hayi yima laosi.
transcription_ft: Míewɔe, gake hiaɖewo yi la, ɖekokutsuviawo wɔa fefe le esi teƒe be woasrɔ̃ nyatefia ŋutɔ. Mesusu be ne wò ŋutsi la dze tɔtrɔ gɔme, abe taɖaƒe tsitsi kple wò gbe ƒe gogblu me ene alo mènye nyateƒe o. Ɖekumenya be ƒleti ya ƒe heyiɣi ma le wosi.
reasoning_base: The text describes a situation where the speaker is unsure about a person's actions and explicitly uses phrases like 'mayang' (we don't know/understand) and 'masusub' (I didn't think/expect), indicating a lack of clarity and understanding of the events described.
reasoning_ft: The text expresses uncertainty and skepticism through phrases such as 'Mesusu be' (I don't think) and 'Ɖekumenya' (I don't know), specifically questioning the truth ('mènye nyateƒe o') of a situation and the conditions of a place.

text: Meɖoa dze kple tasinye elabena eɖea nu geɖewo me nam tso nye dzitsitsi ŋu. Ne dzi ɖe le ƒonye la, Meƒoa bɔl be nye ŋusẽ naɖe le eme. Meŋlɔa nu ɖe ​​ŋkekenyuigbalẽ me ne melé blanui alo nu te ɖe dzinye. Nukae nye SGBV, Aɖaŋuɖola? Esia tae wogblɔ na mí be míagadze amedzrowo yome oa?
transcription_base: Maduadje pletasin yeh, alab na edya nuk gado man nam, chonya chichingo, nagid dalong punya na mawabong banyang usana dalam, manglano da inka kanyi bala mo, na madle blany, alon tada gin yeh, nuk kain yeh SGBV, adang dula, siya tayo gulongan na mibab, miga jeh, amajoro yung oah
transcription_ft: 2 MEDOAD 16. Ne dzi ɖe nèfonye na meƒo abɔ o be nye ŋusẽ nadèle eme. Maŋlãnu ɖe ŋkekenyui gbe le me ne medlɔe bla nyui alo nuteɖedzinye. Nu kae nye esyibibii, aɖaŋuɖola. Esia tae wogblɔna na mí be míegadze amedzrewo yome oa.
reasoning_base: The text mentions 'SGBV' (Sexual and Gender-Based Violence) and describes a difficult situation involving pain and suffering, which strongly indicates a state of sadness and trauma.
reasoning_ft: The speaker expresses worry or apprehension ('meƒo abɔ') regarding their strength ('ŋusẽ') and reflects on the consequences of lack of discipline and the tension that arises when people are pushed too far, suggesting a state of unease or anxiety.

text: Ðe míate ŋu aƒo nu tso nusi woadi tso asinye esi meɖo ƒe 16 la ŋua? Sukudɔdeasi geɖe, agbanɔamedzi geɖe, kple amesiwo biana nusi medi be mawɔ le nye agbe me. Ewɔna nam abe agba gã aɖee wònye ene. Medi be makpɔ egbɔ be nye ŋutɔ tae mele ewɔm, ke menye na dzinyelawo ɖeɖeko o.
transcription_base: Demya tungo ako nyo nusiyaw diyo asing yasimo do, pero yadelangwa. Sukod na si gede, agpano magigede. Kla amas yung biyana nusimadip ba mawaling yagba m. Awananam agba, agba ga aday wonya ene. Madip ba mako e bob, nyo untotay malaawo, kamingyong yagilaawo deta kutaayo.
transcription_ft: Demnya atemwu aƒo nu tso nu si woadi tso asinye esimeɖo bɛ wiaɖe laŋwa, sukudɔdɔasi geɖe agbanɔamɛdzi geɖe kple ame siwo biiana nu si medi be mawɔ le nye agbe me. Ewɔ nànam abe agba gã aɖee wònye ene, medi be makpɔ eƒe gbe nye ŋutɔ tae mele ewɔ, eme nye nyedzi la wo dɛde ko tae o.
reasoning_base: The text contains markers of loss and emptiness. Despite being labeled as Ewe, the vocabulary resembles Northern Philippine languages (e.g., 'awan' meaning 'none/nothing' in Ilocano/Pangasinan and 'pero' for 'but'). Phrases like 'Sukod na si gede' (reached the limit) and 'Awananam agba' (no answer/nothing left) suggest a tone of lament, grief, or sadness.

text: Ele be míafia sɔhɛwo be asixɔxɔ si le wo ŋu mele woƒe ŋutigbalẽ ƒe amadede alo woƒe lãmekawo me o. Ele be wòatrɔ. Hadomegbenɔnɔ ƒe xɔtutu aɖe koe wònye, alo? Ne míetso nya me be dɔmenyonyo kple aɖaŋudzedze nye nu yeye si sɔ la, ekema amesiame akpɔ dzidzɔ wu tsã.

transcription_ft: Mama nye ƒonu tso eŋu si ɖeka eye vovɔ̃a dom ale gbegbe. Ɖematẽ ŋu agblɔ be awo na mama nyea. Mèsena le ɖokuinye me abe ɖe mele gbɛyeye aɖe srɔ̃ egbea ene. Nu sia nu to vovo kura tso nu si wo fiam gbɔ. Nu kae mawɔ ne meyi aƒe me egbea. Akpenawo, tasi.
reasoning_base: The text is a mixture of French and Ewe, referencing 'on dit des cas' (discussing problems/drama) and complex family dynamics involving a mother, babies, a teenager, and an 'ex.' The use of the Ewe word 'tasi' (please/I beg you) at the end indicates a plea to resolve a stressful or difficult situation, which is characteristic of frustration or distress.
reasoning_ft: The speaker explicitly uses the word 'vovɔ̃' (fear) and describes being overwhelmed by it. They express uncertainty and distress about what will happen to their mother and fear regarding returning home, which clearly indicates a state of being afraid and anxious.

text: Mesena le ɖokuinye me be ne me gblɔe na dzinyelawo la, ɖe ko woa xɔ nye nuƒomɔ le asinye keŋkeŋ. Enyo, me le to ɖom. Nukae wo le be matrɔ le alesi me zã nye hadomenyatakakadzraɖoƒewo ŋu? Me susu be me wɔe nenema, gake ɣeaɖewoɣi la amesiwo nye menya o la ga kpɔtɔ ɖoa biabiawo ɖa.

transcription_ft: Mese na le ɖokuenyemɛ be ne megblẽ na dzinye la ɖe ko woa xɔ nyenufomɔ le asinye keŋkeŋ enyo mele to dom, nu kae wòle bematrɔ le ale si mezã nye hadomennya takakaa dzraɖuƒe wò ŋu. Mesusu be mewɔe nenemagakehi aɖewoyi la, ame si wonyem enya o la, gakpɔtɔ ɖoa ababia wo ɖa.
reasoning_base: The text appears to be a hybridized or pseudo-language using Hungarian markers (such as 'nem' for 'not', 'de' for 'but', and 'nyel' relating to 'nyelv' or 'language'). Additionally, the use of plosive and repetitive syllables like 'takaka' and 'gakbododó' often conveys agitation or emotional distress in a linguistic context.

text: Ŋdɔ na wò, Daɖia. Mesusu be enyo ŋutɔ. Suku me yɔ fũ, gake nuwo le ɖoɖoezizi me le aƒeme. Èbe nusiwo míekpɔna gbesiagbe enea? Nusiwo ŋu amewo meƒoa nu tsoe gaglãa ŋutɔŋutɔ oa? Mesusu be alesi wònɔna ɣesiaɣie wònye.

transcription_ft: 1. Melanyoe. Akele nyateƒe mela ame tɔtɔ fie le ŋkeke siawo me. Nye ŋuti lãwoe wɔ kuɖo. Ɣletiv bɛ aɖewoe nye esia me dze asidoɖo anyi. Gakee yɛ sia yi si wòva la, mèsene na le ɖokuinye me abe ame bubu korɔa ene.

text: Ne èdze seselelãme siwo menɔ asiwò tsã o la me tom. Enye ɣeyiɣi si tɔtɔa ame elabena ɣeaɖewoɣi la, mesena le ɖokuinye me abe ŋutsuvi ene gake nye ŋutilã ƒe dzedzeme to vovo. Danye gblɔ be ne nyɔnuviwo dze woƒe ɣleti sia ɣleti asiɖoanyi gɔmee. Enyo, mina míadze egɔme.
transcription_base: Naajasa seolah-olah sioman masih wacana ulama tom. Anya riisy totowo amal abana karuila, mesenale rukunya abang chivi en gakak, gentilah padajamatu wu. Tanya blog na nyonvio jauh pergelatisi aglati asyoran gamai, anyo minamia jaga gam.
transcription_ft: 1 NE ƐDZESƐLƐLƐM 1. Enye ɣeyiɣi si tɔtɔ ame elabena gaɖoɣi la, mèsenale dukunye ame abe ŋutsuvi ene gake, ŋe ŋtila ƒe dzedze me to vovo. Danye blɔ be, Ne nyɔnuviwo dze woƒe ɣleti sia ɣleti asirɔ̃nyigɔmee. Enyo mina míadze egɔme,
reasoning_base: Although labeled as Ewe, the transcription contains several Indonesian keywords such as 'seolah-olah' (as if), 'masih' (still), 'wacana' (discourse/plan), 'ulama' (religious scholars), and 'amal' (deeds). The phrase 'seolah-olah masih wacana' suggests a discrepancy between words/plans and reality, which typically expresses frustration or disappointment regarding a lack of action or implementation.
reasoning_ft: The text describes a difficult or stressful time ('tɔtɔ ame') and mentions physical restlessness or trembling ('to vovo') in the context of a young person dealing with their monthly cycle, which strongly suggests a state of anxiety or being overwhelmed.

text: Ne ŋutsuvi aɖe dze nyɔnuwo ƒe dɔwo wɔwɔ gɔme la, amewo aƒo nu. Eyata, èle gbɔgblɔm be aƒemedɔwo menye esi ŋutsu menye tae o, ke boŋ esi hadomegbenɔnɔ gblɔe tae? Tasɔsɔmasɔwoe? Èdi be yeagblɔ abe alesi Ama mekpɔa ɣeyiɣi sue aɖe tsɔ srɔ̃a nu wu nye enea? Nyemebu eŋu nenema kpɔ o.
transcription_base: Mais beh, des vieux vers sont des derniers au génocide dada. Au secou de derniers dans un son toc et qui a que, c'est assez où, nature attroue et chôme. Preuve fumière et chôme. Ne me bêle, faut fournir à dos du coup. Et globais, mais les tous sont aye, mais n'est accès mandala.
transcription_ft: 2 MEBE 5 Dɛviwo ƒe srɔ̃ɖedɛ nye wodze na ƒe edzi dada. Wosuku dɛdɛ nye dɔwɔnu seawo ŋutɔ kekeake si le asi wò nàtsɔ atrɔ wo etsɔ me kple wo ƒomia ƒe tsɔ me. Ne megbe la, fofonye ado dziku. Egblɔ be, Mele to sem eye menye akpɛmada la.
reasoning_base: The transcription appears to be a phonetic mapping of Ewe speech into French words. Despite the linguistic distortion, the presence of heavy terms like 'génocide', 'chôme' (unemployed/idle), and the phrase 'Ne me bêle' (which phonetically resembles 'Me ble', Ewe for 'I was deceived') suggests a narrative of loss, struggle, or lamentation.
reasoning_ft: The text focuses on the importance of education for children to improve their lives and their families' circumstances. The key phrase 'fofonye ado dziku' translates to 'my father will be proud,' and the overall tone emphasizes self-reliance and achievement.

text: Ena mebiaa ɖokuinye be ɖe míate ŋu atrɔ nusiawo gbeɖeka hã. Ne amesiame le mɔ kpɔm be míawɔ nu le mɔ aɖe nu la, aleke míawɔ adzudzɔ? Gake ne meyi aƒeme hegblɔ na fofonye be eƒe nukpɔkpɔwo do xoxo la, ɖeko wòado dziku. Abe alesi mayi edzi anɔ kpekpem ɖe nɔvinyenyɔnua ŋu enea?

text: Deko wòdzɔna ko. Ŋu kpeam ale gbegbe. Ðe wòdzudzɔna gbeɖekaa? Ewɔna nam abe ɖe amesiame ate ŋu akpɔe ene, togbɔ be medzea agbagba be maɣlae hã. Ke ne nyemeka asi eŋu o ɖe? Ðe wòanɔ nenema tegbeea? Enyo, ema nana nye dzi dzea eme vie.
transcription_base: Dès qu'on jonqu, on prend avec bébé. Dès qu'on agbe des car, et on en a marbre, des amis s'y a mettent et on a coup et on est né. To be be, mais déjà pas be be, mangré hein. Quel neige me cas à six et on ou de. Dès où on en est matig bien. Aïe, on manana nié dit, dia et me vie.
transcription_ft: 1 DEKWO 1. Ŋkpeam ale gbegbe. De wodzɔna gbeɖeka. Ewɔ nànam abe de amesiamete ŋu akpɔe ene. Togbɔ bɛ medzi agbagba bɛ maɣre hɔ. Ke nɛ nyemeka asi eŋu wòɖe. Ɖe wɔanɔ ne nɛma tegbea. Ɛnyo, ema nàna nye edzi dzea eme vie.
reasoning_base: The transcription appears to be a highly fragmented mix of French and Ewe (or a phonetic transcription of a dialect). The presence of the exclamation 'Aïe' (typically indicating pain or distress) combined with references to a 'bébé' (baby) and 'me vie' (my child) and the mention of 'coup' (blow/hit) suggests a tone of distress or grief.

The disjointed nature of the thoughts and the hesitant approach to the situation further indicate confusion.

text: Ame si tsɔa nutefewɔla, dɔmenyotɔ, lɔ̃na be yeasrɔ̃ nu, eye wòdea bubu ame bubuwo ŋu, gake woawo ŋutɔ ƒe taɖodzinuwo hã le wo si. Medi be ame geɖe wu le nye nutoa me nabu tame nenema. Ewɔna nam abe agbanɔamedzi gã aɖe ene, gake mesusu be mele klalo be mate kpɔ. Akpe na wò, Daɖia.
transcription_base: Ama sih, walaupun domanjoto luar bayangan, aye wadah bubur, amabuburun, takak walaupun tak terudinu halo sih, mesti bea amgareu, lenyentu awal nabutama nenema, awal namab, walaupun wala, garen, taka masuk suba melakhalobematik pok, aku nak udari ya.
transcription_ft: 2. ame si yi. wɔ nteƒe, dɔmanyotɔ, lɔna be yea srɔ̃ nu, eye wòdea bubu ame bubuwo nu, gake wɔ wo ŋutɔ ƒe tarodzinuwo hã le wo si. Medi be ame geɖewu le nye nutoa me nabutam ene nema. Ewɔ na nam abe nteƒewɔla gã aɖe ene, ake mesusu be mele khalo be matikpɔ. Akpenawo Daria.

The disjointed structure, combined with phrases suggesting something is beyond the speaker's imagination and a struggle to make sense of a situation, strongly indicates a state of confusion.
reasoning_ft: The speaker is describing someone who is hypocritical—pretending to be hardworking and respectful while actually being a fraudster (nteƒewɔla) and keeping stolen goods (tarodzinuwo). The speaker expresses indignation that the person thinks they are not smart enough to notice the deception, which indicates feelings of anger and betrayal.

text: Ewor nam me be ne megblɔe na dzinyelawo la, ɖeko woaxɔ nye telefon la adzoe keŋkeŋ. Enyo, mele to ɖom. Nukae wòle be matrɔ le alesi mezãa nye hadomenyatakakadzraɖoƒewo ŋu? Mesusu be mewɔe nenema, gake ɣeaɖewoɣi la, ame siwo nyemenya o la gaɖoa nubiagbalẽviwo ɖa.

Mais sous sube, mais où est-né ma gare yadouila, à Messi où n'est Magna, gare doa nous via Banlieue ou da.

reasoning_ft: The input consists solely of a number ('2') and contains no linguistic markers or context to determine an emotion.

text: Danye faa avi ɣeaɖewoɣi, gake egblɔna be yemate ŋu atsi tre ɖe fofonye ŋu o elabena eyae nye aƒea ƒe ta. Amesiame le nye kɔƒea me gblɔna be ne nyemeɖe srɔ̃ kaba o la, mahe ŋukpe vɛ na ƒomea. Woƒoa nu tso sɔhɛmenɔɣi ƒe kɔnyinyiwo kple kornu wo ŋu.
transcription_base: Daniel fávia doy, kake ébrolabé, és már tömöttre de fufonyon o, elapen a, egyené a fiát betá. A messziá meleg kopjá megbrolabé, nem egy medszo kabáola, már hempevéna fomya. Oponutja szobhemé noy, felkonyni ople konum.
transcription_ft: 1 DANYEL 1. Amesia Amalek kɔ fiame gblɔna be, yemade ŋu atsi trɛ ɖa fofonye o elabena eyae nye Afiya Betar. Wo xɔa nu tso sɔb heme no yi ƒe kɔnyinyiwo kple kɔnwoom.
reasoning_base: The text mentions a specific individual (Daniel) and contains phonetic elements resembling words related to burial ('tömöttre' resembling 'tomb') and spiritual longing ('messziá' resembling 'Messiah'), which typically indicates a context of loss, mourning, or grief.
reasoning_ft: The text describes a person (Amalek) who justifies a specific behavior or restriction based on their identity ('because he is Afiya Betar') and mentions the receipt of items during traditional celebrations and anniversaries. This assertion of identity and association with social customs suggests a sense of status or cultural pride, although the overall tone is largely descriptive.

text: Ðeko menɔa anyi hebiaa ɖokuinye be ɖe wòadzudzɔ gbeɖeka hã. Ɣeaɖewoɣi la, mesena le ɖokuinye me be maƒo ame aɖe ne dzi ɖe le ƒonye, ​​abe le suku ene. Mesena le ɖokuinye me be vɔvɔ̃ ɖom. Nuka tututue ema fia? Esi wòwɔe ta la, woɖoe be nye hã mawɔea? Medi be madzudzɔe.

Mesena le adu kpue nyɛ me bɛ fofo dom. Nu ka tototuoɛ mma fie a, asi wowoe ta la, wodroe bɛ nyeha mma woea, mɛdii bɛma dwodwoeɛ.

text: Ne meva zu dɔnɔdzikpɔla la, mate ŋu akpe ɖe nye nutoa me tɔwo ŋu le mɔ si le vevie nam ŋutɔŋutɔ nu. Èsusu be fofonye ase egɔme gbeɖekaa? Enye kɔnyinyi ŋutɔ. Mate ŋu adze agbagba aɖe nusita medi be makpe ɖe amewo ŋu la me. Mesena le ɖokuinye me be mebɔbɔ vie ne meƒo nu tso esia ŋu.
transcription_base: Ne magyarázod, hogy pólá lá matenugaparényen utóan maton legmosolyelve végén amtontani. Az szoba folyik, azt egomabreká, anya pogni intok. Matenugad, jégagbagba arran úsztat, majd be magper a munka. Nem hunko nincs jáulá, anna mepona borít.
transcription_ft: 2 NE 11. Asusu bɛ fofonye ase egɔme gbeɖe kaa. Ɛnye ƒɔnyee tɔ. Mate nu aɖe agba aɖe no sita ame dzi be makpe ɖe amewo la me.

Specifically, the phrase 'Nɔnuvi nui tia ahɔme' indicates that the elder sister is worried or anxious, and the mention of her being unable to speak about a troubling incident further emphasizes a state of distress and anxiety.

transcription_ft: Ame sia ame si le sukufɔa nu tso nutsila aɖe ƒe amesinɔnsɔnɔŋ alo ame kɔkɔnyenye alo nutsigbalẽ aɖe ƒe amesinɔnsɔnɔŋ. Eʋanana mabena me dze nenema o la, ɖeɣoŋo menye nutsi nyuitɔ nenye o. Esiawo to vuvukra tso esiwo mèkpɔna le wògblodoƒe vuƒowo dzi le doa me
reasoning_base: The speaker repeatedly says 'non non non' and explicitly states 'on a plein de problèmes' (we have plenty of problems), and the fragmented, repetitive nature of the speech suggests a state of distress or inability to resolve a situation.
reasoning_ft: The text discusses authority figures (political or religious) and the failure of expectations ('Eʋanana mabena...'), stating that if these are not met, the situation is not good. This critique of leadership and systemic failure conveys a sense of frustration.

text: Ne ŋutsuvi aɖe dze nyɔnuwo ƒe dɔwo wɔwɔ gɔme la, amewo aƒo nu. Eyata, èle gbɔgblɔm be aƒemedɔwo menye esi ŋutsu menye tae o, ke boŋ esi hadomegbenɔnɔ gblɔe tae? Tasɔsɔmasɔwoe? Èdi be yeagblɔ abe alesi Ama mekpɔa ɣeyiɣi sue aɖe tsɔ srɔ̃a nu wu nye enea? Nyemebu eŋu nenema kpɔ o.

reasoning_base: While the text is labeled as Ewe, it contains several words that strongly resemble Indonesian/Malay ('bayar blom' meaning 'not paid yet', 'buah' meaning 'fruit', and 'cahsan' which resembles 'kasihan' meaning 'pity'). text: Ewɔ nam abe ɖe mele agba kpekpe aɖe tsɔm ene. Ðe míate ŋu aƒo nu tso—aleke nèyɔe?—nu siwo ku ɖe 'ƒomedodo nyuiwo' ŋuti le ga me bubua? Akpe na wò, Madam. Akɔfafa gã aɖee wònye nam be menye nye ɖeka koe le nu siawo ŋu bum nyui o.

reasoning_base: The speaker identifies as being unemployed ('chômeur') despite having a qualification ('baccalapar'), and expresses that despite their efforts, nothing is working out or changing ('rien ne recalcule').
reasoning_ft: The speaker expresses deep grief over the loss of family members and feelings of isolation and a lack of support ('no helping hand'), which are characteristic of sadness and mourning.

transcription_ft: Wò susuna be ne lɔnuviwo va le wo ɖokuiwo sia kpã la, nutɔ ame agbã. Mesusu benye dzexɔse nye be elebenu wonono alea le bena wono alea ye sia ɣi. Wo zãa kɔnyinyiwo abe akpɔsɔnu ene be wòatsɔ ana amewɔ naziɖoɖoe. Míedzea egɔm kple nukulele nunu.
reasoning_base: Although the text is labeled as Ewe, it appears to be a distorted transcription of Portuguese. Key terms such as 'coração' (heart) and 'amorona' (derived from 'amor', meaning love), along with 'noalé' (likely referencing 'Noel' or Christmas), strongly suggest a sentiment of love and affection.
reasoning_ft: The text criticizes the exploitation of orphans, stating that they are being used as a facade to solicit donations rather than receiving genuine care and education. This expression of dissatisfaction and moral disagreement with a systemic issue indicates frustration.

text: Egale suku dem kokoko. Egale bɔl ƒom abe ɖe naneke medzɔ o ene. Nukatae wònyea mí ɣesiaɣi? Nukatae wòle be míaxe fe ɖe mí ame evea siaa ta? Mesena le ɖokuinye me be wogbem ale gbegbe, eye nyemegblɔe na wo gɔ̃ hã haɖe o. Mesena le ɖokuinye me be menye ame si woɖe ɖa xoxo.
transcription_base: Pada suku dem koko koko, pada bau rumah di ranah kema jauhnya. Nukatau yang meyesai, nukatau leban, yang kefere, yang mevasiata. Basalah nak kini yang mabuk bang ala baba. Heye, yang mak buenao gahar. Basalah nak kini yang mabuk, menyamise ura rahu.
transcription_ft: Egale sukudeɛm kokoko, egale bɔwo ƒomawo be ɣiri naneke medzɔwoe ene. Nu ka etae wònye mí ye sia ɣi? Nu ka etae wòle ben míaxe feɖe míame evea sia ta. Mèse la nèrɔkyenye me be wogbãm ale gbegbe eye. Ŋimegblɔe na wo gãhã aɖewo. Mèse la nèrɔkyenye me be menya ame si woɖeɖa xoxoxo.
reasoning_base: Although labeled as Ewe, the text contains words strongly resembling Indonesian/Malay (e.g., 'rumah' for house, 'jauhnya' for distance, 'mabuk' for drunk). The themes of being far from home and the repetition of drunkenness suggest a state of longing, regret, or despair, which aligns most closely with sadness.
reasoning_ft: The text is a lamentation expressing deep distress and hardship. The speaker repeatedly asks 'Why is it us?' (Nu ka etae wònye mí ye) and mentions that nothing is going right (naneke medzɔwoe ene), while feeling targeted or misunderstood by others. This combination of questioning fate and describing emotional pain strongly indicates sadness.

text: Shɛli ni be niŋdimi ka di ŋmanila ti be kaaniba npahiri jina ni, dɔmini be bɛla nimmohini n tumda, ti ni tooi wuhiba ti ni bɔhim shɛli kpɛŋɔmaa di ni yɛn niŋ ka be be tɛhi ni ti tamila be yɛla, ka bipʋɣinshɛba ban nyɛ namo nima maa, shɛli ni di gmanila ti tamdila be yɛla. Nbɔri ni n kpaŋla n maŋa nyɛ yilikpɛrilani gahindili.
transcription_base: Shall ne, bukan muka yang malah cuba kahana bapa jenani, datangnya berada di mohon tunda, tuntut wiba tumbuhan syepung sama, diyennan kabupaten tanah cetam laba jera. Kalipu oh shabani namun nama, shall ne datanglah cetam laba jera. Bawa orang panglima yang ikhialang kehandai.
transcription_ft: Shɛli, bɛyuunima o ka ŋmanila tibikahaniba bahi bɛ jinani, dama yɛn bɛ biɛranim mɔhu ni tumda. Tumtum wuhi ba tim bɔhim shɛli kpe ŋ-ŋma din yɛn niŋ ka bɛtɛtɛha ni ti tam la bi yɛla. Ka bipuɣ shɛb bɛn nyɛ namɔnima shɛli din ŋmanila ti tam ni la bi yɛla. Mbooniŋkpamba ŋ-nyɛ yi kpɛlaŋ gahindili,

text: Nsaɣiti ni taɣada nyɛla di ni niŋ dawama ti ʒinɛlipuuni, amaa di ni nyɛ taɣada maa be wuhi ti ni di nyɛla di ni viɛli ti ti biɛhigu bee fukumsi zalisi saɣitili. bɔ nlee nye fukumsi zalisi?
transcription_base: Nsaátí, ne tagadjál a döntő matéri generápulni. Ama döntjél tagadma bővít, ne döntjál döntveit szabog. Be fokomsz zársz sajtol, bo nöntjél fokomsz zársz.
transcription_ft: n-saɣi ti ni taɣa da nyɛla di niŋdawama ti ƒinɛr puuni. Amaa din nyɛ taɣa da ma bɛ wuhi ti ni di nyɛla din viɛti ti biɛhigu bee fukumsi Zalisi saɣi ti li. Bɔ n-lee nyɛ fukumsi Zalisi?

reasoning_ft: The speaker reflects on the fact that the positive aspects of their life are not due to their own efforts ('niŋdawama') but are instead a result of 'fukumsi Zalisi' (the grace of God). Attributing one's well-being to divine grace is a hallmark of gratitude and humility.
